label: Private Nuisance
scenario_patterns: A factory emits noxious fumes that drift into neighbouring residential units
Construction works generate excessive noise and vibration affecting adjacent properties for months
Tree roots from a neighbour's property damage the plaintiff's boundary wall and drainage
A neighbour's persistent burning of incense or joss paper causes smoke to enter the plaintiff's flat
legal_tests: The defendant's use of land caused an unreasonable interference with the plaintiff's use and enjoyment of their land
The plaintiff must have a proprietary interest in the land affected (ownership or exclusive possession)
Unreasonableness is assessed by balancing the gravity of the harm against the utility of the defendant's conduct
Factors include locality, duration and frequency, sensitivity of the plaintiff, and the nature of the interference
The interference may be physical damage to the land or intangible interference (noise, smell, vibration)
common_fact_patterns: Noise from construction, renovation, or industrial activities affecting neighbouring HDB or condo units
Smoke, dust, or fumes from nearby commercial or industrial operations
Water seepage from an upper-floor unit damaging the lower-floor unit in strata-titled property
Tree encroachment — branches or roots from a neighbouring property causing damage
Persistent odour from food establishments affecting residential neighbours
sg_case_references: Tesa Tape Asia Pacific Pte Ltd v Wing Seng Logistics Pte Ltd [2006] SGHC 36 — nuisance from water seepage and interference with use of land
Management Corporation Strata Title Plan No 2668 v Rott George Hugo [2013] SGHC 62 — nuisance in the strata-titled property context
Amritlal Pte Ltd v Ling Kok Lian [2016] SGHC 33 — private nuisance from construction and renovation works
Ng Huat Seng v Munib Mohammad Madni [2017] SGCA 58 — nuisance and encroachment by tree roots
party_roles: landlord of the property causing nuisance
defences: The use was reasonable given the locality and character of the neighbourhood
Statutory authority — the activity was authorised by statute
Prescription — the nuisance has been continuously and openly committed for 20 years
Consent — the plaintiff consented to the interference
Contributory negligence — the plaintiff's own conduct contributed to the harm
